episode_title: How Germany's Ippen Digital is Fine-Tuning Large Language Models for Their Newsroom

codes.description: Ippen's massive corpus of regional/local news content serves as a unique training asset that generic LLMs lack, turning local journalism's historical depth into an AI advantage
excerpts: The idea is leveraging the huge amount of content. especially on a regional and local base that we have, in order to improve the quality of the AI-powered tools
we have a bunch of data that you won't usually find In an LLM out of the box. And of course we want to leverage that those data.
codes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

codes.description: English-trained LLMs systematically fail at German indirect speech (subjunctive vs. indicative), causing incorrect attribution of quotes -- a language-specific error with real journalistic consequences
excerpts: In German, if you like to quote someone in an indirect way, Then you use a specific verb for and this is the subjunctive. And usually it's the indirect speech or reported speech Usually, if you use an LLM out of the box, then you don't get the subjunctive but the indicative.
what happens here is basically you you got rock attributions. You attribute something to someone who basically didn't say that
codes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

codes.description: A deliberate strategic choice to fine-tune open-source rather than proprietary models, requiring commercial licenses and native German pre-training
excerpts: We wanted to have open source, to fine-tune an open source model, not a proprietary model. This model had to offer a commercial license and Very important. It has to be basically this model has to be pre-trained on German language texts. Not just on text or articles that have been translated into German.
codes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

codes.description: The critical distinction between models pre-trained on natively written German text versus translated-into-German text, with the former producing superior linguistic accuracy
excerpts: this model has to be pre-trained on German language texts. Not just on text or articles that have been translated into German.
codes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

codes.description: Building separate fine-tuned models for each specific task (headlines, leads, summaries) rather than using one general-purpose model
excerpts: we started fine-tuning LLMs. to build task specific LLMs. So LLMs that are best suited to reach specific Goal.
If you have, for example, an LLM that is fine-tuned for generating uh headlines. or leads or summaries and so on.
codes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

codes.description: Using Low-Rank Adaptation to fine-tune only a fraction of model parameters, making the process financially accessible to organizations with fewer resources
excerpts: decided to apply an efficient fine-tuning framework That is based on LoRa and basically this enables us to keep costs down.
using an efficient fine-tuning process makes it possible basically to fine-tune just a fraction of the parameters and still achieve very good results.
codes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

codes.description: The empirical observation of a performance peak during fine-tuning where model quality reaches a maximum before potentially declining
excerpts: it was really interesting to see that you really you can experience at some point you experience a leak. in the performance that you get.
codes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

codes.description: Having millions of pre-selected, structured articles ready before fine-tuning is crucial -- data preparation is a prerequisite that determines speed and quality
excerpts: For us it was crucial To us already, we're talking here about millions of articles that we we could use for fine-tuning models. So having data Already pre-selected and structured data that you can use in the right form for this task is crucial.
codes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

codes.description: Using Retrieval Augmented Generation to power reader-facing chatbots that draw only from the organization's own article database, providing context and follow-up information
excerpts: you can use a chatbot that is integrated into the article to ask for additional context and the information that you got are not coming from an LLM. That means are not all the information that you got. But basically this knowledge base is made up of our own articles.
you would like to ask what happened. since then and you have a bunch of article in your archive
codes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

codes.description: Even RAG-based systems can surface outdated information from similar stories, introducing misinformation risk despite source citation
excerpts: if you're just going to create a rag chatbot, there are concerns of hallucination because even if it is pulling out the citations of the appropriate sources It could be pulling out something that is like two weeks old
codes.speaker: Nikita Roy

codes.description: Using autonomous AI agents to double-check information during the writing process against pre-selected reliable external sources
excerpts: Can you make sure that you use an agents-based system to basically Double check the information that you have been ideally during the writing process
It's basically checking If those information are reliable and using external sources that have been already pre-selected because we know they are reliable from our journalistic perspective
codes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

codes.description: The industry wasted time debating whether AGI has been achieved rather than investing in practical AI-assisted workflows
excerpts: I think we spent really last year we spent a lot of time talking about something that I think is a false problem. And there's basically you already rich artificial general intelligence or not. And I think basically we spend far too little time and resources thinking about new AI assisted workflows.
codes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

codes.description: Combining multiple AI tools (transcription, translation, quote extraction, summarization, drafting, self-evaluation) into integrated modular workflows that save significant journalist time
excerpts: I think we think a lot about tool chaining.

You can use AI to transcribe, of course, this text. And you can start translating it. You can extract quotes, you can generate a summary, you can even, if you would like to, you can generate a first draft.
codes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

codes.description: A concrete sports journalism workflow: a 24-minute Jurgen Klopp resignation video processed through AI tool chain replaces hours of manual transcription, translation, and drafting
excerpts: Juven Klump is the German manager of the family club, Liverpool, resigned... His decision was basically explained in a video, it is a 24 minute long video
as a journalist, you have saved a lot of time that you can invest in, for example Writing an opinion piece on this or looking for someone to interview.
codes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

codes.description: AI's primary value is freeing journalists from repetitive tasks so they can reinvest that time in original reporting, opinion, and interviews
excerpts: as a journalist, you have saved a lot of time that you can invest in, for example Writing an opinion piece on this or looking for someone to interview.
we basically build tools to give them the possibility to improve their reporting and basically free them from repetitive tasks and give them time back
codes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

codes.description: The categorical claim that fully automated AI publishing is the fastest way to destroy trust in the media industry
excerpts: fully automating applications, AI applications, are probably the best way to destroy trust in the media industry. So that's why it's really important to have this approach is probably just semi-automated or half automated if you like to.
codes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

codes.description: LLMs exhibit mysterious behaviors -- like adding unwanted summaries at the end of reversioned articles -- that have no clear explanation and must be engineered around
excerpts: we realized that we notice that quite often LLMs tend to add a short summary at the very end As when you reversion articles... And we don't know why. It doesn't make absolutely no sense. No one wants to have a summary at the end of an article.
codes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

codes.description: As LLM output quality improves, editors risk becoming less vigilant in their review, creating a dangerous feedback loop where trust in automation outpaces its reliability
excerpts: The risk is that at some point Editors will get used to high quality results coming from LLM and won't be as careful as they are today in reviewing those outputs coming from LLMs
codes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

codes.description: A technical solution where quotes are first extracted, then the LLM is instructed to preserve them unchanged during content generation, combined with visual highlighting for editor comparison
excerpts: first of all it's extracting quotes And in the first step. And the second step is telling the LLM To perform a task and make sure that those quotes that have been extracted in the first step remain unchanged.
we have been trying to do is making sure that editors don't need to prompt LLMs themselves
codes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

codes.description: An engineering solution that highlights quotes and key information in both original and AI-generated text so editors can visually compare without prompting
excerpts: we have started, for example, highlighting, for example, quotes. Or information in the output text and in the original text So editors can immediately compare the results
codes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

codes.description: The principle that reporters should focus on their core strength (reporting), not prompt engineering, with dedicated prompt editors handling the AI interaction layer
excerpts: you want to make sure that editors don't need to prompt LLMs themselves. They have other strengths. Their strength is our reporting. That's why we have dedicated prompt editors in the team.
codes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

codes.description: The operational reality of scaling AI solutions across 60 news portals, 250 online editors, and nine editorial offices, requiring solutions beyond individual prompting
excerpts: we as Ipm we basically run around sixty News portals or across Germany, we have around 250 online editors across nine editorial offices. So when it comes to scale, a particular use case, you want to make sure that you have a reliable solution.
codes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

codes.description: The difficulty of hiring enough journalists at local/regional level in Germany is a primary motivator for building AI assistance tools
excerpts: We struggle with finding enough journalists on a local level and a regional level.
we don't find enough journalists as we would like to.
codes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

codes.description: Treating an article as a collection of modular parts that can be recombined to serve different story angles, readers, and publications within the network
excerpts: this old concept of modular journalism. It's something we call intelligent content. And it's the idea that you can basically You see an article as a modular piece of content.
if you do it manually, it's an extremely time-consuming task. But now You have the possibility to do it in a reliable way
codes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

codes.description: AI suggests or generates different story angles from a single journalist-written article, allowing the same story to serve different readers and publications differently
excerpts: you can say something like the government coalition is basically losing more and more supporters in Germany You could say this this way. Or you can you could say something like the let's say political party specific political party... is getting more and more traction. These are basically two completely different story angles.
you can, as a political journalist, you can decide Which kind of story angle do you want to get from an LLM?
codes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

codes.description: A JournalismAI Fellowship project that collected 400,000+ Russian state media articles, semantically grouped them into 200 topics, and tracked their spread on social media as an early warning system for manipulated narratives
excerpts: collecting over 400,000 articles from state-run media outlets from Russia. Like Russia today or Sputnik. And we basically selected 25,000 articles about Ukraine and we grouped them semantically into 200 topics
the idea is to use AI and the goal was to use AI to create an early warning system for journalists
codes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

codes.description: A concrete demonstration of LLM danger: when prompted about US biolabs in Ukraine (a known Russian propaganda narrative), early ChatGPT hallucinated a fabricated list of sites
excerpts: I asked ChatGPT to give me a list of uh sites where you can find basically US run biolabs in Ukraine... JetGPT back then basically hallucinated, just made up a list of sites in Ukraine. Where you can't find US run bio labs.
codes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

codes.description: Election periods intensify the need for AI-powered misinformation detection as sympathetic media and coordinated narratives go viral
excerpts: we have elections this year, in several countries, not only in the US, in Germany as well. And I think we have already seen in the last Weeks and days, the risk of sympathetic media going viral.
codes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

codes.description: The non-negotiable principle that all AI-assisted content must begin with a human-written article; AI operates on human-created material, never generates from scratch
excerpts: The starting point is always, we can't stress it enough, always a human-written article. Always.
codes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

codes.description: An emerging tool that recommends relevant information from pre-selected reliable external sources during the writing process, combining RAG and AI agents
excerpts: We have been working on writing assistance for our editors, meaning that during the writing process, an AI-powered system will recommend Relevant information from reliable sources, and here is again combination of rug and agents
codes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

codes.description: Using AI to transform content across formats -- written article to audio to video -- while maintaining guardrails and human oversight
excerpts: starting and different formats as well, written article to audio to video and so on. So starting playing across platforms and across formats But always and I think it's extremely important. Always within very clear guardrails
codes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

codes.description: Alviani built a multi-prompt ChatGPT process to help his girlfriend learn Italian, demonstrating personal investment in AI's educational potential
excerpts: we developed basically a multi-prompt process, let's say this this way, in the ChatGPT app to help her learn additional words and phrases and basically it is a language system for her
codes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

codes.description: The Parrot project's methodology lived on through a US startup (SyncPeople) that expanded analysis to Truth Social and YouTube, showing how fellowship projects can have lasting impact
excerpts: sync people we are still in touch with swap in swapn meta from sync people and they kept refining this methodology and started looking into different social media networks as well. For example Tools Social, or even they started analyzing YouTube as well.
codes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

codes.description: The scale of the model selection process: evaluating over 200 candidate models from thousands of available LLMs before choosing which to fine-tune
excerpts: we listed More than two hundred potential candidates out of several thousand LLMs and we Started fine-tuning
codes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

episode_summary: This episode reveals Ippen Digital as arguably the most technically sophisticated local/regional news AI operation documented in the podcast, pursuing a three-pillar strategy of fine-tuning, RAG, and AI agents that few comparable organizations have attempted simultaneously. The German subjunctive problem serves as a microcosm of a larger truth: English-centric LLMs carry hidden linguistic assumptions that can alter journalistic meaning, making language-specific fine-tuning not a luxury but a factual accuracy requirement. Alviani's most provocative claim -- that the AGI debate is a 'false problem' diverting resources from practical workflow innovation -- positions him against both AI hype and AI panic, advocating instead for the unsexy but high-impact work of tool chaining and modular journalism. The episode surfaces a fundamental tension in automation: AI tools must become good enough that journalists save meaningful time, but not so trusted that editorial vigilance degrades -- a paradox that Ippen addresses through highlighting systems, self-evaluation, and the categorical insistence that a human-written article is always the starting point. The journalist shortage driving Ippen's AI investment reframes AI not as a replacement threat but as a staffing necessity for local journalism's survival.
surprises: Alviani dismisses the AGI debate as a 'false problem' that consumed the industry's attention at the expense of practical workflow development
LLMs mysteriously add unwanted summaries to the end of reversioned articles -- a behavior even the AI team cannot explain
Early ChatGPT hallucinated a list of non-existent US biolabs in Ukraine, directly amplifying a known Russian propaganda narrative
Ippen evaluated over 200 candidate models from thousands of LLMs before selecting ones to fine-tune -- suggesting the selection process itself is a non-trivial competency
The journalist shortage at the local/regional level in Germany reframes AI adoption as a staffing crisis response rather than an efficiency play
Alviani argues editors should NOT learn prompting -- contrary to the prevailing 'everyone should learn to prompt' narrative -- because their strength is reporting
tensions: AI tools must improve enough to save real time, but improving quality risks eroding the editorial vigilance needed to catch remaining errors -- a paradox Alviani explicitly acknowledges
Alviani insists editors should not prompt LLMs (dedicated prompt editors should), while the broader AI literacy movement argues everyone should learn prompting
The modular journalism concept enables personalization but also opens questions about editorial integrity when the same story is served with fundamentally different angles to different audiences
RAG-based chatbots are presented as reliable because they draw from owned content, but the host correctly identifies that even owned content can be outdated, creating a false sense of safety

Fine-tuning on proprietary data creates competitive advantage but also locks the organization into maintaining and updating models as the corpus evolves -- an ongoing resource commitment not fully addressed
The Parrot project tracks state-run propaganda, but the LLMs Ippen uses for production could themselves be vectors for the same kind of misinformation if not carefully constrained
